1: ʾēlleh hadĕbārîm ʾăšer-ṣiwwāh YHWH laʿăśōt ʾōtām: 'These are the words that YHWH has commanded you to do.' Moses assembles the whole congregation to receive the tabernacle instructions. The phrase kol-ʿădat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl (the whole congregation of the children of Israel) emphasizes the communal nature of the project — the tabernacle is Israel's collective act of worship, not Moses's or the craftsmen's alone.
2: šēšet yāmîm tēʿāśeh mĕlāʾkāh ûbayyôm haššĕbîʿî yihyeh lākem qōdeš šabbat šabbātôn laYHWH: 'Six days work shall be done, but the seventh day shall be for you holy, a Sabbath of complete rest to YHWH.' The Sabbath command is placed before the tabernacle construction instructions — even the holy work of building YHWH's dwelling is subordinate to the Sabbath. No project, however sacred, overrides the covenant rhythm of rest. The placement signals that the Sabbath is more fundamental than the sanctuary.
3: lōʾ tĕbaʿărû ʾēš bĕkōl mōšĕbōtêkem bĕyôm haššabbāt: 'You shall not kindle fire in any of your dwelling places on the Sabbath day.' The specific prohibition against fire on the Sabbath — appearing only here in the Torah — may address the smelting and casting involved in tabernacle metalwork. The Sabbath boundary holds even in the productive fire of construction. The Mishnah later derives 39 categories of prohibited Sabbath labor from the 39 types of work performed in building the tabernacle.
4: zeh hadĕbār ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH lēʾmōr: 'This is the thing that YHWH has commanded.' The transition from the Sabbath prohibition to the freewill offering collection. The connection is significant: the offering is described as divinely commanded, not self-initiated by the people — YHWH commands the freewill offering, which means the freedom and the command are not opposed. Commanded generosity is still real generosity.
5: qĕḥû mēʾittĕkem tĕrûmāh laYHWH kōl nĕdîb libbô yĕbiʾehā ʾēt tĕrûmat YHWH: 'Take from among you a contribution to YHWH. Whoever is of a generous heart (kol nĕdîb libbô), let him bring YHWH's contribution.' The contribution (tĕrûmāh, literally 'a lifting up/heave offering') is described by the quality of the giver: nĕdîb lēb (generous/willing heart). The motivation is internal disposition, not external compulsion. The freewill offering theology: the tabernacle is built not by taxation but by the willing generosity of those who have encountered YHWH's grace.
6: The materials list begins: gold, silver, bronze; blue, purple, and scarlet yarns; fine twisted linen; goats' hair. These are the same materials specified in Exodus 25:3-7 — the instructions given on Sinai are now being gathered from the community. The list moves from precious metals to textiles, establishing the hierarchy of value that will be reflected in the graduated holiness of the tabernacle's zones.
7: The list continues: acacia wood, oil for the lamp, spices for the anointing oil and for fragrant incense, onyx stones, and stones for setting in the ephod and breastpiece. The comprehensive list covers everything needed for the completed tabernacle. The anointing oil and incense are specialized — requiring expert preparation (Exodus 30:22-38). The gem-setting requires the craftsman's skill. The community's offering covers both raw materials and specialized items.
8: wĕkōl ḥăkam-lēb bākem yābōʾû wĕyaʿăśû ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH: 'and every skillful person among you shall come and make all that YHWH has commanded.' The skill component: ḥăkam-lēb (wise/skilled of heart) — the craftsman's competence is a matter of the heart, not merely technical training. Wisdom in Hebrew encompasses practical skill as well as moral insight; the skilled craftsman exercises the same faculty as the wise ruler or the discerning judge.
9: The list of structures to be made: the tabernacle and its tent, covering, clasps, frames, bars, pillars, bases; the ark and poles and mercy seat; the table and its poles and utensils and bread of the Presence; the lampstand and its accessories and oil; the altar of incense and its poles; the anointing oil and fragrant incense; the screen for the entrance of the tent; the altar of burnt offering; the court and its equipment; the basin. The comprehensive scope of the tabernacle project is laid before the community: this is what their freewill offering will build.
10: wĕkōl-ḥăkam-lēb bākem yābōʾû wĕyaʿăśû ʾēt kol-ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH: repeated call to skilled craftspeople. The repetition of the call emphasizes that the tabernacle requires both the generous givers and the skilled makers — both are acts of covenant service. The theological connection: spiritual gift and material offering are both responses to YHWH's grace.
11: The construction items: the tabernacle, its tent and covering, its clasps and frames, its bars, pillars, and bases. The technical vocabulary of construction begins here — the terms for the structural elements of the tabernacle will be repeated through chapters 36-40 as their execution is narrated. The repetition (instructions in 25-31, execution in 35-40) embodies the principle that obedient construction follows the divine pattern exactly.
12: The ark with its poles, the mercy seat, and the veil of the screen — the innermost furnishings of the most holy place. The mercy seat (kappōret) is mentioned first in this recapitulation because it is YHWH's throne, the locus of his presence, the theological center of the entire structure. The veil (pārōket) that separates the holy place from the most holy place is the structural boundary between accessible holiness and the unmediated divine presence.
13: The table with its poles, all its utensils, and the bread of the Presence (leḥem happānîm, literally 'bread of faces' or 'bread of the presence'). The twelve loaves placed on the table every Sabbath (Leviticus 24:5-9) represent Israel's twelve tribes perpetually before YHWH's face. The bread of the presence is the enacted sign of Israel's covenant standing: YHWH hosts Israel at his table, sustaining the relationship through continuous provision.
14: The lampstand for light with its accessories and oil for the light. The mĕnôrāh (lampstand) with its seven branches — already specified in Exodus 25:31-40 — is described as providing hammāʾôr (the light). The light of the lampstand in the holy place is the continuous illumination of YHWH's presence — not sunlight but the sanctuary's own light, symbolizing the light of divine revelation in the midst of the community.
15: The altar of incense with its poles, the anointing oil, the fragrant incense, and the screen for the entrance of the tent. The altar of incense (mizbaḥ hamiqṭār) is the innermost accessible altar — standing before the veil to the most holy place. The incense offering represents prayer ascending to YHWH (Psalms 141:2: 'let my prayer be counted as incense before you'). The screen (māsāk) for the entrance separates the holy place from the outer court.
16: The altar of burnt offering with its bronze grating, its poles, and all its utensils; the basin and its stand. The two altars — burnt offering (in the outer court) and incense (in the holy place) — frame the approach to YHWH's presence: first the sacrifice (burnt offering for atonement), then the worship (incense for prayer). The architectural movement inward through the tabernacle is a movement through purification toward intimacy.
17: The hangings of the court, its pillars, their bases, and the screen for the gate of the court — the outer boundary of the sacred space. The court's enclosure defined the perimeter of holiness in the wilderness camp. The gate screen (māsāk) marks the threshold between the camp (ordinary space) and the court (sacred space). The graduated holiness — court, holy place, most holy place — reflects the graduated access to YHWH: all Israel, priests, and high priest once a year.
18: The pegs of the tabernacle and the pegs of the court with their cords. The tent pegs (yĕtēdōt) and cords secure the tabernacle to the ground — the practical anchoring of the divine dwelling in the physical world. The detail is not peripheral: the tabernacle's portability (it can be dismantled and reassembled) is part of its theology — YHWH accompanies his people in their journey, not residing in a fixed location until the land is given.
19: ûgĕdê haśśĕrād laśśārēt baqōdeš bigdê haqqōdeš lĕʾahărōn hakkōhēn ûbigdê bānāyw lĕkahēn: 'the finely worked garments for ministering in the holy place, the holy garments for Aaron the priest, and the garments of his sons, for their service as priests.' The priestly vestments (bigdê šĕrād) are included in the list of items to be provided — the clothing of the mediator is as much a covenant object as the ark or altar. The vestments make the priest who he is in the sanctuary context; they are not mere uniforms but covenantal costume.
20: wayyēṣĕʾû kol-ʿădat bĕnê yiśrāʾēl millipnê mōšeh: 'the whole congregation of the children of Israel departed from Moses's presence.' The assembly disperses to respond individually and communally. The transition from public hearing to private response — from the congregation before Moses to the families at their tents — sets up the extraordinary outpouring of generosity narrated in Exodus 35:21-29.
21: wayyābōʾû kol-ʾîš ʾăšer-nĕśāʾô libbô wĕkōl ʾăšer nādĕbāh rûḥô: 'every man whose heart was lifted up came, and everyone whose spirit moved him.' The two expressions of motivation: nāśāʾ lēb (heart lifted up = motivated, enthusiastic) and nādĕbāh rûḥô (spirit moved = spontaneously generous). Both are interior movements, not external pressure. The offering for the tabernacle is the community's freewill response to YHWH's grace. The language anticipates Ezra 1:5 (the returning exiles whose spirits God stirred to rebuild the temple).
22: wayyābōʾû hāʾănāšîm ʿal-hannāšîm kōl nĕdîb lēb: 'both men and women came; all who were of a willing heart brought.' The inclusion of women as full participants in the offering is notable. The ornaments they bring — clasps, earrings, rings, and pendants, all objects of gold — are the personal jewelry of Egyptian origin (Exodus 12:35-36). The same ornaments stripped off in penitence after the golden calf (Exodus 33:6) are now given for the tabernacle. The redirected wealth of Egypt funds the dwelling of YHWH.
23: wĕkōl ʾîš ʾăšer nimṣāʾ ʾittô tĕkēlet wĕʾargāmān wĕtôlaʿat šānî ûšēš wĕʿizzîm ûʿōrōt ʾēlîm mĕʾoddāmîm wĕʿōrōt tĕḥāšîm ḥēbîʾû: the materials list continues with textiles and hides. The variety of contributors — those who had blue, purple, scarlet, linen, goats' hair, dyed ram skins, sea cow hides — suggests that the community's diverse wealth was comprehensively mobilized. No single type of material came from one source; the tabernacle is a collective offering.
24: kōl-mārîm tĕrûmat kesep ûnĕḥōšet ḥēbîʾû ʾēt tĕrûmat YHWH: 'everyone who made an offering of silver or bronze brought the LORD's contribution.' The silver and bronze offerings — the metals of the court furnishings and altar — come from those who happened to have them. The word mārîm (lifting up/contributing) is the verbal form of tĕrûmāh. Those who had acacia wood contributed it. The matching of resources to need is YHWH's provision through the community's diverse holdings.
25: wĕkōl-ʾiššāh ḥăkmat-lēb bĕyādêhā ṭāwû wayyābîʾû maṭweh ʾet-hattĕkēlet wĕʾet-hāʾargāmān ʾet-tôlaʿat haššānî wĕʾet-haššēš: 'all the women who were skillful of heart spun with their hands, and they brought what they had spun in blue, purple, and scarlet yarns and fine linen.' The skill-and-spirit combination appears in the women's spinning as well as the men's craftsmanship: ḥăkmat-lēb (skillful/wise of heart) is the quality of both Bezalel (Exodus 35:31) and the spinning women. Wisdom and skill are not merely technical — they involve the heart's formation and disposition.
26: wĕkōl-hannāšîm ʾăšer nāśāʾ libbān ʾōtānāh bĕḥokmāh ṭāwû ʾet-hāʿizzîm: 'all the women whose hearts moved them with skill spun the goats' hair.' The goats' hair spinning is distinguished — it is a separate group of women whose hearts moved them with this specific skill. The precision of the distinction shows the narrator attending to the specific contributions of specific groups: not all the women did the same thing; each contributed what she was skilled to give. The community's diverse gifts are all needed and all honored.
27: The leaders (wĕhannĕśîʾîm) brought the onyx stones and the stones for setting in the ephod and breastpiece, and the spices and oil for the lamp, anointing oil, and fragrant incense. The leaders' distinctive contribution is the most valuable materials — the gems and spices that required wealth to obtain. Leadership is expressed here in generous material provision for the community's worship. The leaders' gift completes the comprehensive offering: precious stones and spices that no ordinary member could provide.
28: wĕhabbĕśem wĕhašāmen lĕmāʾôr ûlĕšemen hammišḥāh ûliqṭōret hassammîm: 'and the spice and the oil for the light, for the anointing oil, and for the fragrant incense.' The three specialized uses of oil and spice — lamp oil, anointing oil, incense — require different preparations. The leaders' provision of the raw materials enables the skilled craftsmen to prepare the specific formulations. The community's generosity covers both the materials and the expertise needed to use them correctly.
29: kōl-ʾîš wĕʾiššāh ʾăšer nādab libbām ʾōtām lĕhābîʾ lĕkol-hamĕlāʾkāh ʾăšer ṣiwwāh YHWH laʿăśôt bĕyad mōšeh ḥēbîʾû bĕnê yiśrāʾēl nĕdābāh laYHWH: 'all men and women, the people of Israel, whose heart moved them to bring anything for the work that YHWH had commanded by Moses to be done brought it as a freewill offering to YHWH.' The summary of the offering: the whole community (kol-ʾîš wĕʾiššāh), motivated internally (nādab libbām), brought their contributions as a freewill offering (nĕdābāh) to YHWH. The word nĕdābāh (freewill offering, voluntary gift) is the technical term for an offering given without obligation — here applied to the entire tabernacle construction fund.
30: rĕʾû qārāʾ YHWH bĕšēm bĕṣalʾēl ben-ûrî ben-ḥûr lĕmaṭṭēh yĕhûdāh: 'See, YHWH has called by name Bezalel the son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah.' The divine calling by name — qārāʾ bĕšēm — is the language of prophetic appointment (Isaiah 45:4: 'I call you by your name'). Bezalel's genealogy — son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah — connects him to the leadership present at the Sinai events (Hur stood beside Aaron holding Moses's arms, Exodus 17:12). The appointment of the craftsman is as much a divine calling as the appointment of a prophet.
31: waymallēʾ ʾōtô rûaḥ ʾĕlōhîm bĕḥokmāh bîtĕbûnāh ûbĕdaʿat ûbĕkōl mĕlāʾkāh: 'and he has filled him with the Spirit of God, with wisdom, understanding, and knowledge, and with all craftsmanship.' This is the first explicit charismatic endowment in Scripture — the Spirit of God filling a person for a specific task. The three gifts: ḥokmāh (wisdom — practical skill and discernment), tĕbûnāh (understanding — analytic insight), daʿat (knowledge — experiential and technical know-how). The same triad appears in Proverbs 3:19-20: 'YHWH by wisdom founded the earth, by understanding established the heavens; by his knowledge the deeps broke open.' The craftsman shares in the wisdom by which the world was made.
32: ûlĕḥašōb maḥăšābōt laʿăśôt bazzāhāb ûbakkesep ûbannĕḥōšet: 'to devise artistic designs, to work in gold, silver, and bronze.' The verb ḥāšab (to think/devise/plan) is the same root as the ḥōšēb craftsman whose intricate work is required for the tabernacle. Artistic design is a form of thought — ḥăšōb maḥăšābōt (thinking of thoughts) — and the craftsman's mind engaged in design participates in the same faculty that YHWH used in creation. Beauty is an intellectual as well as a manual achievement.
33: ûbĕḥărōšet ʾeben lĕmallōʾt ûbĕḥărōšet ʿēṣ laʿăśôt bĕkōl mĕleʾket maḥăšābet: 'in cutting stones for setting, and in carving wood, for work in every skilled craft.' The full range of Bezalel's mandate: gem-cutting, wood-carving, and every kind of skilled work. The Hebrew ḥărōšet (craftsmanship, artisanship) from ḥāraš (craftsman) covers the full range of skilled trades. The tabernacle requires all of them — there is no hierarchy among the crafts, only the coordinating direction of the Spirit-filled master craftsman.
34: ûlĕhôrōt nātan bĕlibbô hûʾ wĕʾohŏlîʾāb ben-ʾăḥîsāmāk lĕmaṭṭēh dān: 'and he has given him the ability to teach, both him and Oholiab the son of Ahisamach of the tribe of Dan.' The gift of teaching (lĕhôrōt — from the same root as tôrāh) is included among Bezalel's endowments. The Spirit-filled craftsman is also a teacher — the wisdom given to him is transmissible. Oholiab is introduced as the second master craftsman: from the tribe of Dan (north), pairing with Bezalel from Judah (south) — the collaboration of the two major tribes in building YHWH's dwelling.
35: mallēʾ ʾōtām ḥokmat-lēb laʿăśôt kōl-mĕleʾket ḥārāš wĕḥōšēb ûrōqēm bĕttĕkēlet ûbāʾargāmān bĕtôlaʿat haššānî ûbāššēš wĕʾōrēg ʿōśēh kōl-mĕlāʾkāh wĕḥōšĕbê maḥăšābōt: 'He has filled them with skill to do every sort of work done by an engraver or by a designer or by an embroiderer in blue and purple and scarlet yarns and fine twined linen, or by a weaver — by any sort of workman or skilled designer.' The comprehensive list of craft categories closes with the summary ḥōšĕbê maḥăšābōt (devisers of designs/skilled thinkers). The breadth of Spirit-given skills encompasses every art required for the tabernacle: engraving, designing, embroidering, weaving. The Spirit of God equips the whole spectrum of human artistic capacity in service of the covenant dwelling.
